schools.summary: Moses summoning all Israel to hear the law — 'Moses called all Israel... Hear, O Israel, the statutes and judgments... that ye may learn them, and keep, and do them'; Moses calling not the elders only but as many as could hear, the greatest not above God's command nor the meanest beneath it, the whole nation under the law alike (Henry); here beginning Moses' exposition of the law, opening with a repetition of the ten words of the covenant spoken directly to all Israel (Keil and Delitzsch); presented as a living exhortation, not a mere written record, the statutes spoken 'in your ears this day' (Ellicott); the fourfold charge to learn, keep, and do, hearing to issue in obedience; the word given not for knowledge only but for practice, to learn without doing being to fail of its end.

The covenant of Horeb — 'the LORD our God made a covenant with us in Horeb'; Horeb the same as Sinai, the same mountain with two tops bearing different names, there the decalogue given (Gill); the Law in its very form a covenant, not a bare code of rules but the terms of a relationship between God and his people, sealed by mutual engagement (Ellicott); the Law a peculiar blessing and a very high honour to be taken into covenant by God, the giving of the law an act of grace drawing Israel near as his own, set forth as a covenant-privilege that they might prepare to embrace it (Calvin); the dignity of Israel's standing — the living God condescending to bind himself to a people and them to himself by a covenant, the law the charter of that bond.

The covenant pressed on the living generation — 'the LORD made not this covenant with our fathers, but with us, even us, who are all of us here alive this day'; the words seeming to exclude the fathers, but the Hebrew idiom of drawing a contrast meaning 'not with our fathers only, but with us also,' the covenant a living bond binding those then alive, not a dead thing of a past generation (Clarke, Ellicott, Gill); the present generation in the loins of their fathers when the covenant was made, and so party to it (Ellicott); not all who stood at Sinai now alive (most had died in the wilderness), yet the covenant made with all now alive, with the nation in its continuing life (Wesley); Moses bringing the law home, that each generation own the covenant as personally binding, God's word addressed to every living soul that hears.

debates.question: Does 'not with our fathers, but with us' deny that God made the covenant with the patriarchs or the Sinai generation?

sides.position: no — by the Hebrew idiom of contrast it means 'not with our fathers only, but with us also'; the covenant is a living bond binding the present generation, who were in their fathers' loins, not a dead thing of a past age

themes: not with our fathers, but with us
who are all here alive this day

schools.summary: God speaking face to face — 'the LORD talked with you face to face in the mount out of the midst of the fire'; not the familiar friendly manner in which God spoke with Moses (Ex 33:11), but God's speaking the ten words publicly, audibly, and immediately to the whole people, not by the mouth or ministry of any man (Gill, Wesley); yet they saw no similitude, no visible form (Deut 4:12), heard the very words but beheld no shape, the 'face to face' of the voice not of sight (Ellicott); the directness giving full certainty of the law's divine author, when God openly manifested himself there could be no doubt from whom the law proceeded, the people having it from God's own mouth (Calvin, Wesley); the living God descending in fire and speaking to a whole nation, the law coming with the highest authority.

themes: talked with you face to face
out of the midst of the fire

schools.summary: Moses the mediator — 'I stood between the LORD and you at that time, to shew you the word of the LORD: for ye were afraid by reason of the fire'; Moses acting as a mediator, at the people's own desire, conveying God's word, the Targums reading 'between the Word of the Lord and you' (Wesley, Gill); the reason — the people, terrified by the fire and the voice, dared not draw near nor go up, and so needed one to stand between them and God; not the ten commandments themselves, which God spoke directly to all with his own voice, but the further statutes and judgments given through Moses, the Decalogue immediate, the rest through the mediator (Wesley); the typology — Moses standing between the holy God and the trembling people foreshadowing the true Mediator, Christ, between God and men (1 Tim 2:5, Gal 3:19); the people's fear making a mediator needful, as sinners' fear of God ever does.

themes: I stood between the LORD and you
to shew you the word of the LORD
ye were afraid by reason of the fire
Moses the mediator, type of Christ

schools.summary: The preface of the commandments, redemption the ground of the law — 'I am the LORD thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage'; 'I am the LORD thy God' not a mere introduction but an integral part of the Decalogue, its first part, the declaration of the divine relationship, the covenanted adoption of Israel by God as his own (Ellicott); God claiming obedience as the God who had redeemed them from slavery, grace the spring of duty, redemption the reason of the law; though spoken and written before, the commandments rehearsed again, precept upon precept, line upon line, the law bearing repeating (Henry); the commands in the same order and nearly the same words as Ex 20:1, with a few variations (Gill, Clarke); God first redeeming and then commanding, the law given to a people already saved that they might live as his.

themes: I am the LORD thy God
redemption the ground of the law

schools.summary: The first commandment — 'thou shalt have none other gods before me'; the Hebrew 'upon my face,' in addition to my presence, before my very eyes, and since God is everywhere, in any place where he is — the whole world — no other god may be set up, no spot where his presence is not (Ps 139:7, Ellicott); the command claiming for the LORD the sole place in his people's worship and trust, to have any other god in heart or act being to break it.

Wesley turns the commandment into a searching question for the soul — hast thou worshipped no god but the God of Israel? Is he the God of thy life, the strength of thy heart, the object of thy supreme love and trust? The first commandment is not only against the idols of the heathen but against every rival in the affections — whatever a man loves, fears, or trusts above God is another god before him.

themes: upon my face, in the whole world
God's sole place in the heart

schools.summary: The second commandment — 'thou shalt not make thee any graven image, or any likeness of any thing in heaven, earth, or waters'; nothing in the whole creation to be fashioned into an image of God or made an object of worship, the command sweeping away every visible representation of the invisible God; the ground (drawn from Deut 4:15) — the people saw no similitude when God spoke from the fire, so were not to imagine he could be set forth by any image, to image God being to debase him, mistaking Creator for creature; Wesley pressing the inward sense — the command reaching not only to graven idols but to the images men form of God in their minds, hast thou framed no gross conception but always thought of him as a pure spirit whom no eye hath seen, and worshipped him in spirit and in truth; God to be worshipped as he is, a spirit, not under any form of man's devising.

The jealous God and the visiting of iniquity — 'thou shalt not bow down thyself unto them, nor serve them: for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children'; not the making only but the bowing and serving of images forbidden, the command reaching to every act of religious homage; God jealous for his own honour and his people's undivided worship, not giving his glory to another, idolatry provoking his jealousy as a wife's unfaithfulness her husband; no sins so surely entail penal consequences on after-generations as the abominations of idolatry, which degrade the divine image in man and pass their corruption down the line (Ellicott).

Calvin against the evasions of idolaters — they vainly try to elude this command by their cavils, as the Papists with their trifling distinction between latria (worship due to God) and dulia (a lesser veneration given to images and saints); God forbids all bowing and serving of images, and no subtle distinction escapes the command.

themes: nor bow down nor serve them

schools.summary: The mercy to those that love God — 'shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love me and keep my commandments'; over against the visiting of iniquity to the third and fourth generation stands mercy to thousands, the promise of grace far outrunning the threat of judgment, God more ready to bless the faithful than to punish the rebellious; the marks of those to whom mercy is shown — 'them that love me and keep my commandments,' love and obedience joined, the keeping of the commandments the fruit and proof of love to God; the echo in Christ's words 'If ye love me, keep my commandments' (John 14:15), the same union of love and obedience running from the law into the gospel, Christ promising the presence of the Comforter to those who so love and obey (Ellicott); God's mercy for those whose obedience springs from love, not slavish fear.

The third commandment — 'thou shalt not take the name of the LORD thy God in vain; for the LORD will not hold him guiltless'; literally 'thou shalt not put the name of Jehovah to vanity,' to anything false, hollow, or unreal, the name not to be linked to falsehood or emptiness; primarily false swearing — calling God to witness a lie, or breaking an oath taken in his name — but the principle extending to every light, profane, or empty use of the holy name (Ellicott); the solemn sanction — though men make light of profane speech, God will surely call it to account, the guilt of dishonouring his name not going unpunished; the name of God, in which all his glory is summed, to be used always with awe.

Wesley presses the conscience — hast thou never used the name of God but on weighty occasions, and then with the deepest awe? Hast thou honoured his word, his ordinances, his name? The command reaches to all irreverent speech, careless oaths, and the profaning of holy things.

themes: not take the name in vain
the holy name used with awe

schools.summary: The fourth commandment, with 'keep' for 'remember' — 'keep the sabbath day to sanctify it, as the LORD thy God hath commanded thee'; here 'keep the sabbath day to sanctify it,' while in Ex 20:8 'remember the sabbath day to keep it,' the two forms of one substance — both to remember the day and to keep it holy, the one word calling to bear it in mind, the other to observe it in deed (Gill); the added clause 'as the LORD thy God hath commanded thee' pointing back to the command already given, the Sabbath no new thing but a standing ordinance; the day to be kept and sanctified, set apart from common labour for rest and holy exercises, hallowed to God.

Wesley presses the conscience — dost thou do no work on this day which could be done as well on another? Art thou careful to avoid all converse and business that does not tend to the knowledge and love of God? The command reaching to the manner of the heart's keeping, not the bare ceasing from toil.

themes: keep the sabbath day to sanctify it
as the LORD hath commanded thee

schools.summary: The Sabbath rest for servants and beasts — 'the seventh day is the sabbath of the LORD thy God... that thy manservant and thy maidservant may rest as well as thou'; the rest not for the master only but for all under his roof and charge — the bondservant, maidservant, stranger within the gates, and the labouring beasts; the Exodus form speaking more generally, while here the ox and ass are particularly named, that they too might rest from their toil, the Deuteronomy version drawing out the mercy of the command toward the lowly and the dumb (Gill); God's law caring for the rest of the servant and the beast, that none in Israel however mean should be worked without respite, the Sabbath a weekly mercy to the whole household; fitting in Deuteronomy, where the reason given is Israel's own former bondage; a humane as well as a holy ordinance.

themes: the seventh day is the sabbath
that thy manservant may rest as well as thou
a weekly mercy to the household

schools.summary: Redemption as the Deuteronomy reason for the Sabbath — 'remember that thou wast a servant in the land of Egypt... therefore the LORD thy God commanded thee to keep the sabbath day'; whereas in Ex 20:11 the reason is that God rested on the seventh day from creation, here Moses presses another reason — that Israel, having been slaves in Egypt and denied all rest, and now redeemed by God's mighty hand, should the more readily grant rest to their own servants and keep the day in thankful remembrance of their deliverance (Clarke, Gill); the reason bearing especially on the mercy of the command toward servants, a people who knew the misery of bondage bound to give their servants the weekly rest they were once denied; the two reasons, creation and redemption, not at odds but completing each other, the Sabbath commemorating both God's finishing of his works and his freeing of his people; the gospel pattern — the rest kept in remembrance of redemption, as the Christian rest looks to the finished work of Christ.

themes: thou wast a servant in Egypt

schools.summary: The fifth commandment, the first with promise — 'honour thy father and thy mother... that thy days may be prolonged, and that it may go well with thee'; the command to revere, obey, love, and care for those who gave us life, the first of the commandments concerning our duty to man; the first commandment with promise, as Paul notes (Eph 6:2), the promise of long life and happiness in the land annexed to keeping it, the added words 'that it may go well with thee' found here and cited by Paul (Gill, Ellicott); God crowning the honouring of parents with long life and well-being, for the duty preserves the order of families and society, on which a people's welfare rests; to honour parents being to honour God who set them over us.

Wesley presses the conscience — hast thou not been irreverent or undutiful to either parent? Hast thou slighted their advice, or cheerfully obeyed all their lawful commands? Hast thou loved and honoured their persons, and supplied their wants? The command reaching to reverence, obedience, affection, and support.

themes: honour thy father and thy mother
that thy days may be prolonged

schools.summary: The sixth commandment — 'thou shalt not kill'; the unlawful taking of a man's life, murder, forbidden, the command guarding the sacredness of human life, which God made in his own image; here in Deuteronomy the latter commandments joined by the copulative 'and,' binding them one to another and to the foregoing, the whole law seen as one (Gill).

Wesley presses the conscience — hast thou not tempted any to what might shorten his life? Tempted none to intemperance, nor suffered any to be intemperate under thy roof? Done all thou couldst to preserve the life of all? The command forbids not only the act of murder but all that tends to it, reaching to anger, hatred, and every neglect of a neighbour's life, as our Lord taught (Mt 5:21); binding us not only to do no murder but to do all we can to preserve life, positive as well as negative.

The seventh commandment — 'neither shalt thou commit adultery'; the violation of the marriage covenant, and by extension all unchastity, forbidden, the command guarding the sanctity of marriage, the purity of the person, and the order of the family; Wesley pressing the inward application after our Lord — the command reaching not to the outward act only but to the heart and the eye, if thou hast not been guilty of any act of uncleanness yet hath thy heart conceived no unclean thought? hast thou not looked on a woman so as to lust after her, betraying thine own soul? — for Christ taught that whoso looketh on a woman to lust after her hath committed adultery already in his heart (Mt 5:28); the command binding the thoughts and desires as well as the deeds, requiring purity of heart and eye; God guarding the marriage bond as a sacred thing.

themes: reaching to the heart and eye

schools.summary: The eighth commandment — 'neither shalt thou steal'; the taking of what is another's, by force, fraud, or any wrong, forbidden, the command guarding every man's right to his own and binding us to honesty in all our dealings; Wesley pressing the application to the root — hast thou considered that the very houses, lands, money, and goods thou callest thine own are not thine own but belong to another, even God, the sole proprietor of all? — so that we are but stewards of what we hold, and to misuse, hoard, or withhold from God and the needy their due is itself a kind of theft; the command forbidding not only open robbery but all dishonesty, overreaching, withholding of wages, and unfaithful stewardship, and binding us positively to deal justly and use what we have as God's trust.

The ninth commandment — 'neither shalt thou bear false witness against thy neighbour'; the giving of false testimony, especially in judgment, forbidden, the command guarding a man's good name and the truth between man and man, on which justice and trust depend; Wesley pressing the breadth far beyond the witness-box — hast thou not been guilty of evil-speaking, of needlessly repeating the real fault of thy neighbour? for if thou see a man do an evil thing and tell it to another, unless from real necessity or to do good, thou breakest this command; forbidding not only the sworn lie but all slander, backbiting, tale-bearing, and the needless spreading of a neighbour's faults; binding us to truth and charity in all our speech of others, to guard his good name as our own; much of common talk, the careless repeating of others' failings, falling under this command.

themes: neither shalt thou bear false witness

schools.summary: The tenth commandment, with the wife first and the field added — 'neither shalt thou desire thy neighbour's wife, neither shalt thou covet thy neighbour's house, his field...'; the command forbidding not the act only but the inward desire of what is another's, going deepest of all, to the heart's wish, convicting of sin where no outward deed appears (Rom 7:7); the variations from Exodus — here the wife put first, before the house, whereas Exodus names the house first (Gill); 'his field' added here, not in Ex 20:17, fitting because Israel had now become or were about to become landowners in Canaan (Clarke, Ellicott); 'desire' used of the wife and 'covet' of the goods, marking the wife as a person not a mere possession; Wesley's sense — thou shalt not desire anything not thine own, and why shouldst thou, seeing God hath given thee whatever tends to thy good, contentment the cure of covetousness; the tenth commandment, reaching the heart, showing the spiritual nature of the whole law.

themes: neither shalt thou desire thy neighbour's wife
nor covet his house, his field
the desire of the heart forbidden

schools.summary: The Decalogue complete, written on two tables — 'these words the LORD spake... with a great voice: and he added no more. And he wrote them in two tables of stone'; the ten words spoken audibly by the Lord himself, heard by the whole congregation (Gill); a dispensation of terror, the law given amid fire and darkness, designed to make the gospel of grace the more welcome and to be a specimen of the terrors of the judgment-day (Heb 12:18, Wesley); 'he added no more' meaning either that God spoke no more in that immediate awful manner (the rest of the law coming through Moses), or that there were only ten commandments, the Decalogue complete in itself (Ellicott); God manifesting his presence by sure tokens, that there be no doubt of the law's divine authority (Calvin); the commandments, spoken to the ear, also written by God's own hand on stone, that they might endure, the abiding charter of the covenant committed to Moses.

themes: wrote them in two tables of stone

schools.summary: The people's terror at the voice — 'when ye heard the voice out of the midst of the darkness... ye came near unto me, even all the heads of your tribes, and your elders'; the people, overwhelmed by the awful voice and the burning mountain wrapped in thick darkness, drawing near to Moses, the heads and elders coming forward as representatives of all, unable to bear the immediate presence of God; the consternation so great that they could not endure that God should speak more to them directly, and owned their need of a mediator (Henry); Moses reminding them of their own petition, lest afterward they undervalue his teaching as merely a man's, for his mediation was at their own request, born of their fear (Calvin); the law given amid such terror showing the people their distance from the holy God and their need of a mediator, the dread of Sinai preparing the way for the gospel and for Christ (Heb 12:18); sinful men unable to bear the unveiled presence of God.

themes: the voice out of the midst of the darkness
the terror drives them to a mediator

schools.summary: The people's confession of God's glory — 'the LORD our God hath shewed us his glory and his greatness... we have seen this day that God doth talk with man, and he liveth'; owning the glory and majesty God displayed in descending on the mount and giving the law with awful solemnity (Gill); marvelling that, having heard the very voice of God out of the fire, they were yet alive, for they thought no man could see or hear God and live (Ex 33:20), a wonder of mercy that they were spared; by their confession binding themselves to obedience, for confessing they saw God's glory they laid on themselves the necessity of obeying, unless they would convict themselves of ingratitude, their own words a pledge against them if they should rebel (Calvin); those who have seen and confessed the greatness of God the more bound to obey him, the confession of his glory carrying the obligation of obedience.

themes: shewed us his glory and his greatness
heard his voice out of the fire
God doth talk with man, and he liveth

schools.summary: The people's fear of death — 'why should we die? for this great fire will consume us: if we hear the voice of the LORD our God any more, then we shall die'; being now alive and wonderfully escaped, they would not expose themselves again to the danger, fearing the great fire would consume them if the awful speaking continued (Gill); though God had for this once kept them alive, they could never endure any farther discourse from him in so terrible a manner, but would surely sink under the burden of it (Wesley); the truth in their fear — sinful and mortal man cannot stand before the unveiled majesty and holiness of God, their dread the just sense of the creature before the Creator and of the sinner before the Holy One; this fear driving them to seek a mediator, the terror of the law preparing the way for the mercy of a go-between, and ultimately for Christ.

themes: this great fire will consume us
if we hear the voice any more

schools.summary: The wonder that they heard God and lived — 'who is there of all flesh, that hath heard the voice of the living God speaking out of the midst of the fire, as we have, and lived?'; no man in any age ever known to hear God speak so directly and live, a thing without parallel that a whole nation should hear the very voice of God and not be consumed (Gill); 'flesh' used with a sense of lowliness and frailty, for man in his frail, corruptible, mortal state, set over against 'the living God,' the contrast between weak dying man and the eternal life-giving God (Calvin, Wesley); pure mercy that preserved them, the holiness of the living God might justly have consumed frail flesh that drew so near, only his forbearance sparing them; feeling their frailty before the living God, they rightly judged they could not bear to hear him again, and sought a mediator; the gulf between the living God and dying man bridged only by God's mercy and a go-between.

themes: who is there of all flesh
the voice of the living God
frail flesh before the living God

schools.summary: The people's request for a mediator — 'Go thou near, and hear all that the LORD our God shall say... and we will hear it, and do it'; asking Moses to go up to the mount and to God upon it, and to receive the divine word in their stead, unable to bear God's immediate voice, they would have Moses stand between and report what God spoke (Gill); the promise joined — to hear and obey whatever God should say through Moses, owning Moses as the appointed channel; feeling their frailty and fear, they rightly sought a mediator and rightly bound themselves to obey, the asking good and approved by God; the typology — the seeking of a mediator to go between them and the holy God foreshadowing the office of Christ, the one Mediator, through whom alone sinners draw near and receive God's word (1 Tim 2:5, Heb 12:24); the promise to obey, though sincere, proving beyond their strength.

themes: we will hear it, and do it

schools.summary: God's approval of the people's request — 'the LORD heard the voice of your words... they have well said all that they have spoken'; God hearing them not merely in the general way he hears all that men speak, but with approbation, owning their request as good (Gill); God himself pronouncing their words good — their fear of his immediate voice, their seeking of a mediator, and their promise to obey all approved; God not despising the people's fear nor rejecting their request, but accepting it and providing the mediation they sought, meeting their weakness with condescension, speaking thenceforth through Moses; while approving their words, God yet knowing the inconstancy of their hearts, soon to wish for in them a heart to match their profession; the request for a mediator according to God's own mind, who had ever purposed to come to his people through a go-between.

themes: the LORD heard the voice of your words
God owns the office of mediator

schools.summary: God's lament over the people's heart — 'O that there were such an heart in them, that they would fear me, and keep all my commandments always'; God expressing, after the manner of men, a longing that the people might have a heart answerable to their fair profession; they would not prove so firm in keeping their promise as ready to make it, hypocrisy not yet purged from their minds, their words good but their hearts unstable (Calvin); the people lacking, and unable of themselves to produce, the new heart the keeping of the law required, the law commanding obedience but not creating the heart to obey, the verse pointing beyond itself to the promise of a new heart under the gospel (Jer 31:33, Ezek 36:26).

Gill: there are not properly such volitions or unfulfilled wishes in God, who works all his pleasure; the Scripture speaks after the language of men, partly to upbraid the people's want of heart, partly to show what is pleasing to him. Clarke renders it 'Who will give that they had such a heart?' — the people refused to receive such a heart from God, and who then can supply it but God himself? Wesley takes it as a true token that God earnestly desires the salvation of sinners.

debates.question: How is God's wish 'O that there were such an heart in them' to be understood, given his sovereignty and the people's inability?

sides.position: a way of speaking after the manner of men — not a properly unfulfilled wish in God (who works all his pleasure), but an upbraiding of the people's want of heart and a declaration of what pleases him, revealing both man's inability to make himself a new heart and God's gracious desire (the new heart given only under the gospel, Jer 31:33)

rendered as a question, 'Who will give that they had such a heart?' — the people refuse to receive such a heart from God, and none but God can supply it; yet the words show God truly and earnestly desires the salvation of sinners

themes: O that there were such an heart in them
the people lack a heart to obey
the promise of a new heart

schools.summary: The people sent back to their tents — 'Go say to them, Get you into your tents again'; the people having been brought by Moses, at God's direction, to the foot of Mount Sinai to receive the law from God's own mouth, that being done and their request for a mediator granted, they are ordered to return to their tents, their part at the mount finished (Gill); by sending them home and keeping Moses with him, God plainly subjoins his consent to the people's prayer for a mediator, ratifying their request by his own decree, the dismissal the seal of his approval (Calvin); since God himself appointed Moses as the channel of his word, for the people afterward to refuse to obey Moses would be perverseness and rebellion against God's own arrangement, in despising the mediator despising the God who gave him; God sparing the people the terror of his immediate voice, meeting their frailty with kindness.

themes: get you into your tents again
to reject the mediator is to reject God

schools.summary: Moses kept to receive the law — 'stand thou here by me, and I will speak unto thee all the commandments... which thou shalt teach them'; Moses, unlike the people, not permitted to return to his tent but ordered to remain and stand by the Lord upon the mount, to wait upon him and receive his instructions, the mediator abiding in God's presence to learn his will (Gill); God giving Moses the whole body of the law, the further statutes and judgments beyond the ten words, that Moses might teach them to the people, the mediator receiving from God and delivering to the people; Moses standing between, hearing from God what the people could not bear to hear, and teaching them, the pattern of all true prophecy and ministry; the honour and burden of standing alone in God's presence where the people dared not; the typology — Moses foreshadowing Christ, who is in the bosom of the Father and has declared him (John 1:18).

themes: I will speak unto thee all the commandments

schools.summary: The charge to obey without swerving — 'ye shall observe to do therefore as the LORD your God hath commanded you: ye shall not turn aside to the right hand or to the left'; to observe every precept, both as to its matter and its manner, doing all that God commanded and in the way he commanded it, under a sense of the obligation his giving of the law laid on them (Gill); the way of truth and righteousness a right line, a man to walk straight on, declining neither to excess on one hand nor defect on the other, to swerve either way being to leave the path (Clarke); the charge binding the people not only to embrace the law but to be content with its pure and unadulterated teaching, receiving as right whatever God commanded, neither adding nor taking away (Calvin); true obedience keeping the straight line, neither turning aside to the right by superstition and will-worship, nor to the left by neglect and license; he who marks not God's word never fulfilling his will.

themes: observe to do as the LORD commanded
turn not to the right or the left

schools.summary: The promise of life in the way of obedience — 'ye shall walk in all the ways which the LORD your God hath commanded you, that ye may live, and that it may be well with you, and that ye may prolong your days in the land'; none of God's commanded ways to be avoided or departed from on any consideration, the people to walk in the whole compass of his commandments, not in some chosen part (Gill); God never gave a commandment he did not design man should obey, and he who selects only the precepts he feels little inclination to transgress, neglecting the rest, deceives himself, partial obedience being no obedience (Clarke); the threefold promise — life, well-being, and length of days in the land the reward of obedience, the way of God's commandments the way of blessing and life; the law promising life to those who kept it (Lev 18:5, Rom 10:5), though none could so keep it as to earn it, pointing to Christ who kept it for his people; obedience and blessing joined in God's ordering.

themes: walk in all the ways God commanded
it may be well with you
obedience the way of life and blessing
